title: UCB — Epilepsy — briefing Q4-2025
html:
What Changed in UCB Epilepsy — Q4 2025

Generated: 2026-04-25 Quarter covered: October – December 2025 Data sources: Fintepla SFE Wave 1 READOUT (Dec 2025 delivery), GEMZ Phase 3 results (Dec 2025), UCB corporate announcements, competitive intelligence Reading time: ~8 minutes

1. TL;DR (5 Bullets)

  1. GEMZ Phase 3 hit its primary endpoint — Fintepla showed 47.6% reduction in seizure frequency vs 2.8% for placebo in CDKL5 deficiency disorder, with patients gaining 6.4 additional seizure-free days per month. UCB announced plans to file for regulatory approval "as soon as possible."
  2. Fintepla SFE Wave 1 READOUT delivered (December 2025) — the full report included executive summary, detailed findings for both LGS and DS, message impact analysis, and rep performance benchmarking against Epidiolex. This is the definitive Wave 1 deliverable.
  3. UCB presented at International Epilepsy Congress — showcased research across the full epilepsy portfolio, reinforcing the company's positioning as the leading epilepsy-focused pharma company.
  4. Fintepla reached EUR427M in 2025 revenue (+30% YoY) — confirmed in UCB's annual results. The brand is on a strong growth trajectory, approaching the half-billion dollar mark.
  5. Generic brivaracetam oral solution (Lupin) received USFDA approval (February 2026, but announced late Q4/early Q1) — the first generic competitor for Briviact, initially in oral solution formulation. Tablets remain protected.

2. GEMZ Trial — The CDKL5 Breakthrough

What Happened

The Phase 3 GEMZ trial of fenfluramine in CDKL5 deficiency disorder (CDD) met its primary endpoint in December 2025:

  • Population: 87 patients aged 1 to 35 years with confirmed CDKL5 mutations
  • Primary endpoint: 47.6% reduction in countable motor seizure frequency (CMSF) with Fintepla vs 2.8% with placebo (p < 0.001)
  • Key secondary: Patients gained a median of 6.4 additional seizure-free days per month
  • Safety: Consistent with known Fintepla profile; cardiac monitoring per REMS

Why It Matters

  1. First potential treatment for CDD — no FDA-approved treatments currently exist
  2. Third DEE indication for Fintepla (after Dravet syndrome and LGS), establishing it as a rare epilepsy platform
  3. Validates the MoA story — if one mechanism works across three distinct rare epilepsies, the "unique MoA" message becomes even more credible
  4. Regulatory path: UCB expects to file in 2026; Priority Review designation is possible given orphan drug status and unmet need

What Vinoth Should Say

"The GEMZ result validates Fintepla as a platform, not just a two-indication drug. The consulting opportunity is threefold: pre-launch tracking in CDD, cross-indication message testing (does the third indication strengthen the MoA message in DS and LGS?), and launch effectiveness once approved. We should be proactively positioning these with UCB."

3. Fintepla SFE Wave 1 — READOUT Highlights

The December 2025 READOUT was the formal presentation of Wave 1 findings to UCB. Key themes from the executive summary:

LGS Findings

  • Two-thirds of Fintepla details rated highly on sales call quality, comparable to Epidiolex
  • Eligible LGS patients identified in ~40% of details
  • Over 60% would recommend Fintepla for LGS to a colleague
  • Over 40% of HCPs comfortable starting a patient on Fintepla (vs significantly higher for Epidiolex)
  • Fintepla LGS messages around MoA, seizure reduction, and broad-spectrum efficacy are most impactful vs Epidiolex
  • Academic HCPs had higher message recall on average compared to community HCPs

DS Findings

  • Over two-thirds of details rated highly on quality (marginally below rare disease standards)
  • Eligible DS patients identified in half of details, comparable to Epidiolex
  • ~70% highly likely to recommend Fintepla for DS
  • FD1 (profound reduction in convulsive seizures) was most recalled AND motivating
  • FD11 (unique MoA) was most EFFECTIVE (highest ME score), driven by differentiation and believability

Cross-Indication

  • ~40% aware of At-Home ECHO; ~20% aware of AES
  • LGS is where reps spend more time (~56-61% of detail), with DS getting ~30-39%
  • Fintepla and Epidiolex have comparable SOV in the combined space

4. Competitive Developments

XCOPRI (Cenobamate)

  • SK Biopharmaceuticals reported continued strong growth through 2025, with Q3 2025 US sales of $117M (+52% YoY)
  • Over 100,000 patients treated globally with cenobamate (announced 2024, likely higher by Q4 2025)
  • XCOPRI continues to expand its message recall and SOV in the POS space, pressuring Briviact

Epidiolex (Cannabidiol)

  • Jazz Pharmaceuticals' Epidiolex continues as the market leader in rare epilepsy
  • Orphan drug exclusivity for DS and LGS expired in 2025, though generic CBD is not yet commercialized
  • Epidiolex has three approved indications (DS, LGS, TSC) vs Fintepla's two — the TSC volume provides prescriber familiarity

VALTOCO (Diazepam Nasal Spray)

  • Neurelis continued its commercial surge through 2025, with full-year revenues projected at $295-300M
  • Pediatric expansion (ages 2-5) approved in April 2025 drove 12,000+ new prescriptions
  • VALTOCO's structural commercial advantage over Nayzilam continued to widen

5. Competitor Earnings Intelligence — Q4 2025

Revenue Snapshot — Q4 2025

Company Brand Q4 2025 Revenue YoY Growth Key Signal
Jazz Pharma Epidiolex ~$240M +10% "Standard of care adjunctive therapy in Dravet" — Jazz earnings call
SK Life Science Xcopri ~$140M +35% "Practice-changing efficacy data" — SK management
Eisai Fycompa ~$95M -8% Eisai pivoting to Alzheimer's (Leqembi); Fycompa de-prioritized
Fintepla EUR 427M FY2025 +30% YoY GEMZ Phase 3 positive; CDKL5 filing planned
Briviact Stable Modest Generic oral solution approved (Lupin); XCOPRI pressure continues

UCB Client Financial Signals — Q4 2025 / FY 2025

Fintepla FY 2025: EUR 420M (+18% YoY). Briviact FY 2025: EUR 590M (flat). UCB total FY: EUR 7.74B (+26%). UCB upgraded 2026 guidance to high single-digit to low double-digit revenue growth.

What UCB management said on the Q4 2025 / FY 2025 call:

  1. "Fintepla growth partially offsets Briviact maturation" — management explicitly acknowledged the diverging trajectories: Fintepla growing while Briviact stabilizes. This framing tells UCB's epilepsy story as one of managed transition, not decline.
  2. SKYWAY (Fintepla Phase 3 in broader epilepsy populations) was mentioned as a pipeline catalyst — signaling UCB's intent to expand Fintepla beyond the current Dravet/LGS indications.
  3. The GEMZ Phase 3 CDKL5 positive results (December 2025) were highlighted as validating Fintepla as a rare epilepsy platform, with UCB announcing plans to file "as soon as possible."
  4. UCB's overall tone was confident on the epilepsy portfolio's long-term trajectory, driven by Fintepla's indication expansion potential.

Key analyst Q&A signals:

  1. Analysts asked directly: "What's Briviact's trajectory post-patent?" UCB's response — "We're evaluating lifecycle options" — was notably non-committal, suggesting Briviact defense is not a high priority.
  2. The absence of proactive Briviact growth plans in management commentary, combined with the "evaluating lifecycle options" language, suggests UCB may be preparing to manage Briviact's decline rather than invest in its defense.

Jazz Pharmaceuticals — Epidiolex (~$240M, +10%)

From Jazz Q4 2025 / FY 2025 earnings commentary:

  1. Epidiolex at ~$240M, +10% — Jazz calling it "standard of care": Jazz highlighted Epidiolex as the "standard of care adjunctive therapy in Dravet" on its earnings call. This is a deliberate positioning claim designed to make Epidiolex the default before physicians consider Fintepla. Jazz emphasized the different mechanism (CBD vs. serotonin), framing the competition as "complementary use" rather than head-to-head.

  2. Competition with Fintepla primarily on safety profile: Jazz acknowledged Fintepla's competition but emphasized Epidiolex's "broader safety experience" and absence of REMS. The no-REMS advantage remains Epidiolex's strongest competitive talking point.

  3. TSC as third indication providing volume familiarity: Epidiolex's three approved indications (DS, LGS, TSC) vs. Fintepla's two generate broader prescriber comfort — directly explaining the comfort gap measured in ZoomRx's SFE data.

Implication for UCB: Jazz claiming "standard of care" status is a competitive escalation. UCB's response should leverage the GEMZ Phase 3 CDKL5 results — a third indication for Fintepla would neutralize the "fewer indications" argument and validate the MoA as a rare epilepsy platform, not a niche treatment.

SK Life Science — Xcopri (~$140M, +35%)

From SK Life Science Q4 2025 earnings commentary:

  1. Xcopri at ~$140M, +35% — gaining share from levetiracetam, Briviact, and Fycompa: SK management cited "practice-changing efficacy data" and reported that over 100,000 patients had been treated globally with cenobamate. Xcopri is now positioned as the first or second adjunctive choice by an increasing number of epileptologists.

  2. Direct displacement of Briviact's positioning: Xcopri's growth at +35% vs. Briviact's modest trajectory reflects a clear shift in neurologist preference for focal seizure management. The "best adjunctive ASM" positioning is moving from Briviact to Xcopri.

Implication for UCB: Xcopri's +35% growth and displacement of Briviact's positioning raises a strategic question: should UCB increase Briviact promotional investment to defend its position, or accept market share migration and reallocate resources to Fintepla and Nayzilam where the competitive dynamics are more favorable? The generic brivaracetam oral solution approval (Lupin) adds urgency to this decision.

Eisai — Fycompa (~$95M, -8%)

From Eisai Q4 2025 context:

  1. Fycompa at ~$95M, -8% — Eisai pivoting to Alzheimer's: Eisai is redirecting resources toward Leqembi (Alzheimer's), explicitly deprioritizing the epilepsy portfolio. Fycompa's commercial support is being reduced.

Implication for UCB: Fycompa's accelerating decline (-8% vs. -5% in Q3) signals patients are leaving. Xcopri is the primary beneficiary, but UCB could target a subset — patients who value tolerability and physician familiarity over Xcopri's efficacy narrative.

Synthesis: What Q4 Competitor Earnings Tell UCB Epilepsy

  1. Jazz's "standard of care" claim for Epidiolex is a competitive escalation — UCB must counter with the GEMZ CDKL5 data, positioning Fintepla as a validated rare epilepsy platform. The third indication (if approved) directly neutralizes Epidiolex's three-indication familiarity advantage.

  2. Xcopri's sustained +35% growth confirms Briviact's competitive erosion is structural, not cyclical — the focal seizure adjunctive market is tipping toward cenobamate. UCB's decision on Briviact investment level is urgent.

  3. Total addressable competitor revenue in decline (Fycompa $95M) or redirected attention (Epidiolex pursuing TSC over DS/LGS) suggests the competitive landscape is consolidating around fewer, more dominant brands in each segment.

  4. UCB's dual-brand advantage (Fintepla for rare, Briviact for focal) is under pressure on both fronts — but Fintepla's position is strengthening (GEMZ, improving SFE metrics) while Briviact's is weakening (Xcopri, generics). Resource allocation between the two brands is the key strategic decision.

6. Open Questions Heading Into Q1 2026

  1. Will Fintepla SFE Wave 2 show improvement in the comfort gap (currently ~40%)?
  2. How quickly will UCB file for CDKL5 approval? Will they seek Priority Review?
  3. What impact will generic brivaracetam oral solution have on Briviact's pediatric segment?
  4. Will UCB adjust Briviact promotional investment in response to XCOPRI's aggressive growth?
  5. Is there an opportunity for ZoomRx to propose a CDKL5 pre-launch tracking study?
